UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Stores user-specific settings, preferences, and a reference to their external Google account for the Automaton Edge application.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity.

googleId.description: The unique identifier from the external Google authentication system associated with this user profile.

preferredPersistence.description: User's preference for data storage, e.g., 'local', 'firestore', or 'hybrid' for cross-device synchronization.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user profile was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user profile was last updated.

AutomationTask.title: AutomationTask
AutomationTask.description: Represents a user-defined configuration for a browser automation workflow, created from a natural language prompt.

id.description: Unique identifier for the AutomationTask entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile entity that owns this automation task. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N AutomationTask)

name.description: A user-friendly name or title for the automation task.

prompt.description: The natural language input provided by the user to define the automation task.

configuration.description: A JSON string storing detailed configuration parameters for the AI automation core, including strategies for survey completion and anti-disqualification.

status.description: The current lifecycle status of the automation task (e.g., 'draft', 'active', 'paused', 'archived').

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the automation task was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the automation task was last updated.

AutomationLogEntry.title: AutomationLogEntry
AutomationLogEntry.description: Records the details and outcome of a single execution instance of an AutomationTask, forming the automation history log.

id.description: Unique identifier for the AutomationLogEntry entity.

automationTaskId.description: Reference to the AutomationTask entity that was executed for this log entry. (Relationship: AutomationTask 1:N AutomationLogEntry)

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile entity that initiated the automation task, useful for direct querying of user logs. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N AutomationLogEntry)

startTime.description: Timestamp indicating when the automation execution started.

endTime.description: Timestamp indicating when the automation execution finished.

outcome.description: The final result of the automation execution (e.g., 'success', 'failed', 'halted', 'disqualified', 'completed_with_notes').

details.description: A comprehensive string containing a summary, specific output, error messages, or alerts from the automation run.

contextualSnapshots.description: An array of URIs or text snippets representing key web page contexts or survey questions analyzed by the AI during execution.

visualizerLogUri.description: An optional URI pointing to a detailed log or recording of the real-time action visualizer for this execution.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when this log entry was recorded, useful for auditing.

definition.description: Stores individual user profiles. The document ID for each UserProfile is the Firebase Authentication UID of the owning user, enabling direct path-based authorization. Includes denormalized 'id' (as document ID) for authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier (Firebase Auth UID) of the user who owns this profile.

definition.description: Stores user-defined browser automation task configurations. Nested under the user's profile for clear ownership. Includes denormalized 'userId' field for authorization independence, allowing direct ownership checks without cross-document 'get()' calls.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier (Firebase Auth UID) of the user who owns this automation task.

params.name: automationTaskId
params.description: The unique identifier for the specific automation task.

definition.description: Records details and outcomes of a single execution instance of an AutomationTask. Nested under the specific automation task and user for clear hierarchy. Includes denormalized 'userId' field for authorization independence, allowing direct ownership checks without cross-document 'get()' calls.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier (Firebase Auth UID) of the user who owns this log entry.

params.name: automationTaskId
params.description: The unique identifier of the AutomationTask associated with this log entry.

params.name: logEntryId
params.description: The unique identifier for the specific automation log entry.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure is designed with a strong emphasis on Authorization Independence, Structural Segregation, and clear Access Modeling, ensuring robust, debuggable security rules and efficient data access patterns. The core of the design revolves around nesting user-specific data under a `/users/{userId}` path, where `{userId}` directly corresponds to the Firebase Authentication UID.

1.  **User Profiles (`/users/{userId}`):** User profiles are stored as top-level documents within the `users` collection. The document ID for each UserProfile document is the Firebase Authentication `uid` of the owning user. This inherently makes each profile privately accessible only by its owner (e.g., `request.auth.uid == userId` in rules), fulfilling Path-Based ownership for private data. This design also provides Authorization Independence as the `userId` is directly available in the path, making it self-authenticating.

2.  **Automation Tasks (`/users/{userId}/automationTasks/{automationTaskId}`):** Automation tasks are nested as a subcollection under the respective user's profile. This hierarchical path (`/users/{userId}/automationTasks`) clearly establishes a 1:N relationship where a user owns multiple automation tasks. Critically, each `AutomationTask` document will *include a `userId` field* that explicitly stores the owner's UID. This denormalization is crucial for **Authorization Independence**. Security rules for an `AutomationTask` can directly check `request.auth.uid == resource.data.userId` without needing to perform a `get()` operation on the parent user document. This also enables **QAPs (Query-Based Access Patterns)**, as a user can securely list all their tasks by querying `automationTasks` within their user path, and the rules will ensure only their tasks are returned.

3.  **Automation Log Entries (`/users/{userId}/automationTasks/{automationTaskId}/logEntries/{logEntryId}`):** Log entries, representing individual executions of an automation task, are further nested under the specific automation task they belong to. This continues the hierarchical path structure for nested 1:N data (`/users/{userId}/automationTasks/{automationTaskId}/logEntries`). Similar to `AutomationTask`, each `AutomationLogEntry` document will *include a `userId` field* (denormalized from the owning user profile). This ensures that authorization rules for log entries are also **Authorization Independent**, allowing direct checks like `request.auth.uid == resource.data.userId` without `get()` calls to the parent task or user documents. This design allows for efficient listing of log entries for a specific task or for all tasks belonging to a user, fully supporting **QAPs** by ensuring that rules enforce ownership based on the `userId` in the path and the document data.

**Overall Authorization Independence:** By consistently denormalizing the `userId` into `AutomationTask` and `AutomationLogEntry` documents, and leveraging the `userId` in the path segment for user profiles, all authorization checks can be performed locally on the document being accessed or its immediate path, entirely eliminating the need for `get()` calls in security rules. This makes rules simpler, more performant, and atomic. The structural segregation ensures that all documents within a given collection path (`/users/{userId}/automationTasks` or `/users/{userId}/automationTasks/{automationTaskId}/logEntries`) share the same security posture, making rule writing straightforward and highly debuggable.
